definition: A predefined price level where a trade is exited if the setup is invalidated. It is placed on swing highs/lows, order block edges, or breaker block boundaries to limit losses. The stop loss determines risk amount and position size, and should be set so that price wicking the level does not close a still-valid trade.
identification_rules: For a long entry, place the stop on the swing low below the order block or on the lower end of the order block.
For a short entry, place the stop on the swing high above the order block or on the upper end of the order block.
When entering at the mean threshold of an order block, keep the stop at the same swing high/low rather than tightening to the order block edge.
The stop must sit beyond the invalidation point so that price wicking the order block does not close the trade if the setup is still valid.
In a Fair Value Gap entry, place the stop outside the sweep high/low that triggered the entry.
In a Breaker Block trade, place the stop either tight to the breaker block edge or at the nearest swing high/low.
If Breaker Block and FVG overlap (Unicorn Model), stop can be placed outside the FVG or breaker block boundary.
In Candle Three Closure entries, switch to lower timeframe and place stop below the most recent protected swing low.
Calculate position size using: contracts = target risk amount / (point value × stop distance in points).
trading_implication: Stop loss placement directly controls risk-to-reward. A tight stop (e.g., order block edge) yields higher R:R but risks being wicked out on still-valid trades. A wider stop (swing high/low) is safer but requires a farther take profit to maintain R:R. The preferred approach is to keep the stop on the swing and adjust the entry closer to invalidation. Stop distance also determines position size: wider stops reduce contract count for the same dollar risk.
common_mistakes: Placing the stop on the order block low/high to improve R:R, then getting wicked out on a single tick even though the trade was never truly invalidated.
Moving the invalidation point closer to the entry instead of moving the entry closer to a fixed invalidation point.
Using fixed contract sizes instead of adjusting position size based on stop distance, leading to inconsistent risk per trade.
Placing stop at swing low without adjusting take profit to maintain R:R, resulting in lower actual R:R.
Entering without a valid model signal, leaving no structural support for stop placement.
merge_notes: All three input cards agree on school (Risk Management). No school conflict. The Foundation card provides the most detailed identification rules and common mistakes, which are preserved. ICT and ICT Updated cards contribute complementary phrasing on stop placement logic.
